mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 (340) engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F-Type 3.0 V6 (AJ126) engine typically costs £5,500–£9,000 supply only. Customers have reported dealer quotes of around £15,000–£30,000 for engine replacement. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 (340) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ126 is a fantastic engine — the V6 and V8 engines are globally reliable — but it has well-documented weaknesses. Plastic coolant pipe failure (particularly the 'V pipe' under the supercharger) is the most common cause of catastrophic engine failure. Timing chain guide wear is a known design flaw. Water pump failure can strike early. Proper maintenance and proactive replacement of plastic pipes with aluminium upgrades are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 (340)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plastic coolant pipe failure (the 'V pipe' under the supercharger — the most critical issue), timing chain guide and tensioner wear, water pump failure, supercharger coupler wear, and fuel injector failure. See the Era Map above for detailed advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 (340) engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept F-Type (values £20,000–£40,000+ depending on year and mileage), a £5,500–£7,000 reconditioned engine is 15–35% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a solid example. The Jaguar scrap threshold of 60% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing. Given the F-Type's status as a future classic, engine replacement is often worth it for a good example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar F-Type 3.0 V6 (340) engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code AJ126 is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 340 PS V6 was the entry-level engine from 2013–2020. In 2017, it was replaced by the 380 PS V6 in some markets, but the 340 PS remained available. The 340 PS V6 was discontinued in 2020 when the Ingenium four-cylinder replaced it. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the AJ126 timing chain, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain, guide and tensioner replacement costs £1,500–£4,000. However, if the chain skips and valves meet pistons, a full engine replacement (£5,500–£9,000) is usually required. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340) Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the F‑Type 340 PS AJ126 era (2013–2020). Jaguar F‑Type V6 engine replacements typically cost £5,500–£9,000. Customers have reported dealer quotes of around £15,000–£30,000 for engine replacement.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an F‑Type 3.0 V6 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The supercharger and V6 layout add complexity. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340) engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide & Tensioner Wear (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic clattering from the front of the engine, restricted performance warning, loss of power, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,000 (if chain skips and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The AJ126's timing chain system is the engine's Achilles' heel. A well‑known design flaw sees the steel tensioner piston wear against an aluminium button on the guide, slowly wearing it away over time. If you're lucky, symptoms may start with a rattle on cold start or a restricted performance warning. But sometimes they will spontaneously fail, leaving no option other than a replacement engine or a potentially more expensive full rebuild including valves and pistons. A cold‑start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston‑valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes are the best prevention.

problems.title: Plastic Coolant Pipe Failure (AJ126 — "V Pipe")
problems.symptoms: Sudden coolant loss, overheating, steam from engine bay, sweet smell, low coolant warning, bubbling in coolant reservoir.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or overheating, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: The most common cause of catastrophic engine failure on the AJ126 is the plastic coolant "V pipe" under the supercharger cracking and dumping coolant. One owner described it as "a well‑known weak spot on these engines that often fails catastrophically". The accepted problem with plastic coolant pipes is that they become brittle due to repeated heat cycles. If the pipe fails while driving, the system dumps most of its coolant through the failed pipe, leading to rapid overheating and often a cracked cylinder head. Many specialists recommend replacing the plastic pipes with aluminium upgrades proactively.

problems.title: Water Pump Failure (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Low coolant level warning, coolant leak around the water pump pulley, sweet smell from engine bay, overheating.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss is detected

problems.recommendation: Water pumps on early supercharged models are prone to failure — sometimes they last less than 20,000 miles. This is because coolant gets inside the bearing housing, corroding the water pump bearing and causing excessive wear. Symptoms will likely be a low coolant level warning and traces of a coolant leak around the water pump pulley. Luckily, the water pump on the V6 is fairly easy to access at the front of the engine. We also recommend replacing the small coolant pipe that connects to the pump at the same time, as it has very fragile plastic connections that will likely break upon removal.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Loud rattling or clunking noise from the top of the engine at cold idle, noise reducing as RPM increases, clonk when shutting the engine down.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can lead to supercharger damage

problems.recommendation: The supercharger drive coupling on the AJ126 can wear, with the holes in the coupling becoming slightly elongated and rattling at idle as the engine RPMs are uneven. The classic symptom is a loud rattle from the top of the engine at cold start. To check, disconnect the drive belt and rock the pulley forwards and back to check for play. Replacement couplers are available relatively cheaply, but fitting requires supercharger removal. At the first sign of rattling from the supercharger area, have the coupler inspected.

problems.title: Fuel Injector Failure (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, white smoke from exhaust, fuel smell, loss of power, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,000 (if injector failure leads to engine damage or catalytic converter failure)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or white smoke occurs

problems.recommendation: Injector failure is a known issue on the AJ126. A leaking injector can allow fuel spray to bypass the rings and enter the oil sump, contaminating the oil and causing wear on engine components. Over time, moisture builds up in the cylinder head and causes oxidisation of the aluminium where the injector seats. One owner reported a leaking injector that also "killed the catalytic converter". If you experience rough running, misfires or white smoke, have the injectors inspected immediately. Replacing a faulty injector before it fails completely can prevent catalytic converter damage.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Jaguar F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340) — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement
Proactive aluminium upgrade prevents catastrophic failure
Straightforward replacement; catch it before overheating
Catch it before catalytic converter damage occurs
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340)? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes
* Plastic coolant pipes replaced with aluminium upgrades
* Water pump replaced (especially on early cars)
* No cold‑start rattle — timing chain guides should be healthy
* CPO or extended warranty in place
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — the AJ126 is unforgiving of neglect
* Any cold‑start rattle — timing chain work is imminent and expensive
* Coolant loss with no diagnosis — plastic pipes may be failing
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the AJ126 can hide expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Jaguar F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340) represents the entry point into Jaguar's modern sports car — a glorious supercharged V6 with 340 PS, 450 Nm and a soundtrack to die for. The AJ126 is a fantastic engine with one of the best supercharger whines in the business. However, it demands respect: plastic coolant pipe failure (particularly the "V pipe") is the most common cause of catastrophic engine failure, timing chain guide wear is a well‑known design flaw, and water pump failure can strike early. Buy on service history, prioritise a car with aluminium coolant pipe upgrades, and budget for preventative maintenance. As one owner put it: "The F‑Type is an addictive beast. It is the most beautiful, most engaging, most fun, and most interesting car on the road". Just make sure you can afford to keep it that way.

cta.label: → Compare F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340) engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar F‑Type 3.0 V6 (340) (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle from the front of the engine (timing chain guide warning) or top of the engine (supercharger coupler)
* Service History — Regular oil changes are essential for the AJ126
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates plastic coolant pipe or water pump issues
* Coolant Pipe Upgrade — Has the car had aluminium coolant pipes fitted? If not, budget for this
* Water Pump — Any history of water pump replacement? (early cars are prone to failure)
* Supercharger Noise — Any rattling on cold start? Coupler wear is common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Timing, cooling, or injector codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to injector or catalytic converter issues)
